one_line_takeaway: By comparing target-vs-reference-model probabilities only at the tokens a fine-tuned LLM finds hardest to predict, rather than aggregating over the whole sequence, HT-MIA extracts a sharper membership-inference signal that beats seven baselines on AUC in 3 of 4 datasets (losing only to the simpler Ratio baseline on IMDB), and this signal is substantially, though not completely, suppressed by DP-SGD fine-tuning.
key_finding: On Asclepius, HT-MIA achieves the highest AUC on all three target models (LLaMA-3.2-1B 73.48%, Qwen-3-0.6B 73.12%, GPT-2 56.79%), beating the strongest baseline (Ratio) by roughly 5-6% AUC on the two larger models, and reaches 4x the TPR of Min-K++ at strict FPR=0.01 on LLaMA-3.2-1B (8.17% vs 1.82%... actually reported as 'nearly 4x'). On Clinicalnotes, HT-MIA again leads on AUC for all three models (86.59% LLaMA-3.2-1B, 88.43% Qwen-3-0.6B, 69.78% GPT-2), with the largest margin on GPT-2 (+5.38% over Ratio's 64.40%). On Wikipedia, HT-MIA leads with 86.2% (LLaMA-3.2-1B) and 91.0% (Qwen-3-0.6B) AUC, beating Ratio by 4.7% and 7.3% respectively, with especially large TPR gains (5-15x) at very low FPR (<0.01). On IMDB, HT-MIA is the ONE dataset where it does not win overall AUC: Ratio outperforms it (95.9% vs 92.3% on LLaMA-3.2-1B; 97.4% vs 94.7% on Qwen-3-0.6B), though HT-MIA still beats all other baselines and remains competitive with Ratio at very low FPR (<0.001). The ablation shows the mechanism behind the method: on a Clinicalnotes example, ~91% of the hardest (lowest-confidence) tokens show positive probability improvement (target > reference) for a member sample versus only ~70% for a non-member sample -- a distinction the authors say is diluted when probabilities are aggregated over the whole sequence, explaining why sequence-level baselines (Loss, Zlib) underperform. As a defense, DP-SGD fine-tuning on Asclepius reduced HT-MIA's AUC from 0.5679 to 0.5008 (11.8% relative reduction) on GPT-2 and from 0.7312 to 0.4957 (32.2% relative reduction, i.e., to near-chance) on Qwen-3-0.6B, with similar reductions observed for the Loss, Lowercase, Ratio, and Zlib baselines.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is HT-MIA (Hard-Token Membership Inference Attack), a token-level membership inference attack against fine-tuned LLMs. Instead of aggregating prediction statistics (loss, perplexity) over an entire input sequence, HT-MIA performs teacher-forced forward passes of the same text through the fine-tuned target model F and its pre-trained reference model B, adaptively selects the K tokens with the lowest predicted probability under F (K = min(max_k, max(min_k, floor(alpha*L)))), computes the per-token probability difference Delta_i = p_F,i - p_B,i only at those low-confidence tokens, and outputs the membership score as the fraction of selected tokens where Delta_i > 0. A sample is classified as a member if this score exceeds a threshold tau. The paper also evaluates DP-SGD as a defense within the same framework. NOTE: the abstract text supplied in the source record ('...unimodal and multimodal membership inference attacks leveraging internal llm states...') does NOT describe this paper's method -- that phrase actually describes LUMIA (Ibanez-Lissen et al., 2024), a paper cited in this paper's own Related Work section as prior work on internal hidden-state MIAs. This review is grounded in the actual PDF full text, not that mismatched abstract string.
threat_model: Adversary targets a fine-tuned LLM F (fine-tuning setting, not pre-training) and has black-box-style query access sufficient to obtain per-token output probabilities/logits for arbitrary input sequences via teacher-forced next-token prediction. The adversary also has access to (or can approximate) a reference model B -- either the exact pre-trained base model used before fine-tuning, or, in a more practical variant tested by the authors, a different but related pre-trained model (Qwen2.5-0.5B or DistilGPT-2 substituted as reference when the exact pre-trained base is unavailable). Goal: given a text sequence x, decide whether x was a member of the fine-tuning training split Dmem or the non-member split Dnon-mem, using a per-token probability-improvement signal S(x;F,B) thresholded at tau chosen for a target false-positive rate.
limitations: Findings self-reported by the paper's own text: (1) HT-MIA does not achieve the best overall AUC on IMDB, where Ratio's simple full-sequence likelihood ratio wins by a clear margin (95.9%/97.4% vs. 92.3%/94.7%), attributed by the authors to IMDB's short, formulaic reviews producing uniformly strong signals that favor simpler ratio-based methods over adaptive token selection. (2) On Clinicalnotes at the relaxed FPR=0.1 threshold, Ratio narrowly beats HT-MIA on LLaMA-3.2-1B (70.70% vs 70.57%). (3) GPT-2 (the smallest model) shows consistently weaker absolute membership signal than the larger Qwen-3-0.6B and LLaMA-3.2-1B across all datasets, which the authors attribute to lower memorization capacity. (4) The attack setting assumes access to per-token output probabilities/logits via teacher-forced forward passes and a usable reference model (either the exact pre-trained base or a close substitute) -- this is a fairly strong access assumption compared to purely black-box generation-only access. (5) The paper evaluates only one defense (DP-SGD) and does not test adaptive attackers that are aware of the hard-token selection strategy.

datasets_used: Augmented Clinical Notes (Bonnet et al., 2023) -- referred to as 'Clinicalnotes'
Asclepius Synthetic Clinical Notes (Kweon et al., 2023) -- referred to as 'Asclepius'
IMDB Large Movie Review dataset (Maas et al., 2011)
quantitative_results: Asclepius AUC: HT-MIA 73.48% (LLaMA-3.2-1B), 73.12% (Qwen-3-0.6B), 56.79% (GPT-2) -- highest of all methods on all three models
Asclepius TPR@FPR=0.1: HT-MIA 37.07% (LLaMA-3.2-1B) vs 23.30% for next-best (Ratio); 31.29% (Qwen-3-0.6B)
Clinicalnotes AUC: HT-MIA 86.59% (LLaMA-3.2-1B), 88.43% (Qwen-3-0.6B), 69.78% (GPT-2) -- highest on all three; Ratio next-best at 86.42%, 86.81%, 64.40% respectively
Clinicalnotes TPR@FPR=0.1: HT-MIA 70.57% (LLaMA-3.2-1B, narrowly below Ratio's 70.70%), 68.61% (Qwen-3-0.6B), 32.23% (GPT-2)
Clinicalnotes TPR@FPR=0.01: HT-MIA 42.53% (LLaMA-3.2-1B), 38.39% (Qwen-3-0.6B), 5.24% (GPT-2) -- highest of all methods on all three
Wikipedia AUC (ROC/Fig.2): HT-MIA 86.2% (LLaMA-3.2-1B) and 91.0% (Qwen-3-0.6B) vs Ratio 81.5% and 83.7%
IMDB AUC (ROC/Fig.2): Ratio wins with 95.9% (LLaMA-3.2-1B) and 97.4% (Qwen-3-0.6B); HT-MIA is second at 92.3% and 94.7%

Ablation (Clinicalnotes, Qwen-3-0.6B example): ~91% of selected hard tokens show positive target-vs-reference probability improvement for a member sample vs ~70% for a non-member sample; example HT-MIA scores shown as 0.958 (member) and 0.850 (non-member)
DP-SGD defense on Asclepius: HT-MIA AUC drops from 0.5679 to 0.5008 (11.8% relative reduction) on GPT-2, and from 0.7312 to 0.4957 (32.2% relative reduction, effectively chance-level) on Qwen-3-0.6B
baselines_compared: SPV-MIA (Fu et al., 2023) -- perturbation-based, self-prompt calibration
